draft.introduction: The Lokasenna (“Loki’s Flyting”) is an Eddic poem in which Loki crashes a feast of the gods and systematically insults each deity, exposing their hidden shames. It dramatizes Loki’s role as truth-teller and chaos-bringer.

translation.text: Then Thor spoke: “Be silent, perverse creature! My mighty hammer Mjǫlnir shall stop your speech. I shall strike your skull from your neck, and then your life shall be ended.” Loki spoke: “I have spoken before the Aesir what my spirit urged me. But before you alone I shall go out, for I know that you do strike.”
translation.original_text: Þá kvað Þórr: “Þgi þú, rágs vrættr! Þér skal Mjǫlnir mál of fyrða; hraða klett ék þér hals af nám.”

translator_notes.note: Loki yields only to Thor’s threat of physical violence, not to argument—the gods cannot refute his accusations because they are true. The Lokasenna is unique in Indo-European mythology for its sustained internal critique of the divine order, anticipating the gods’ own destruction at Ragnarǫk.
